title: Committed review keeps PATCH-shape concern in observations alongside omitted-config state drift

provenance.originSummary: Synthetic benchmark scenario designed to exercise the rule directly.
provenance.whyItMattered: Use this case to benchmark that committed review preserves both an atypical PUT-versus-update-shape concern and an omitted-config read-state drift concern on the same new-resource PR, keeping the former as a non-blocking observation instead of dropping it once a blocking issue exists.
provenance.genericCondition: Sanitized fixture models a new typed provider resource whose create path uses a full PUT-style request, whose update path calls the create-style helper even though the SDK exposes a dedicated update path, and whose read path hydrates API-returned metadata into an Optional field even when the config omits it.

description: A sanitized committed-review scenario for a new typed resource family where the review must simultaneously surface an atypical create-versus-update request-shaping concern in `OBSERVATIONS` and an omitted-config state-drift risk in `ISSUES`.

scope.notes: Sanitized fixture models a new typed provider resource whose create path uses a full PUT-style request, whose update path calls the create-style helper even though the SDK exposes a dedicated update path, and whose read path hydrates API-returned metadata into an Optional field even when the config omits it.

mustCatch.description: The committed review should surface the atypical create-versus-update request-shaping concern in `OBSERVATIONS` when the SDK exposes a dedicated update path but current-run evidence does not yet prove a blocking field-loss or service-contract failure.
The committed review should surface the read-state drift risk when an Optional field omitted from config is repopulated from API-owned metadata during refresh.

mustNotFlag.description: The final review must not collapse to only the state-drift issue while silently dropping the separate create-versus-update request-shaping concern when the fixture evidence supports both.

notes: Use this case to benchmark that committed review preserves both an atypical create-versus-update request-shaping concern in `OBSERVATIONS` and an omitted-config read-state drift concern in `ISSUES` on the same new-resource PR instead of oscillating between whichever one the reviewer notices first or dropping the non-blocking concern entirely.
